speaker: interviewee
text: Can I have some water?

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes, yes, yes.

speaker: interviewee
text: Do I need to put lipstick on?

speaker: interviewer
text: No.

speaker: interviewee
text: Because I don't want to look like--

speaker: interviewer
text: No, you look gorgeous.

speaker: interviewee
text: --I don't want to look like death warmed over.

speaker: interviewer
text: Don't start.

speaker: interviewer
text: You look gorgeous. You look great.

speaker: interviewer
text: Don't start yet. Let her drink water. Drink. Just--

speaker: interviewee
text: No, I am fine. It's OK.

speaker: interviewer
text: You want to relax a little bit?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. It's all right.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. So we was talking about Plaszow, about the roll calls. And it's very interesting to know if you heard about some selections going on in Plaszow, to set people--

speaker: interviewee
text: I was there.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell us-- tell us--

speaker: interviewee
text: I was there in many selections.

speaker: interviewer
text: --tell us something about the selections.

speaker: interviewee
text: First of all, there was one selection that men and women were examined-- so-called examined. You just paraded in front of the doctor. And the doctor sent you to the left or to the right. And this was one of the selections. This was-- many, many people were taken out at that time and sent away, I think, to Mauthausen, men to Mauthausen, and women, probably, to Auschwitz. One selection, I will remember very vividly. One time, they told us to go all on the roll-call square. And as we were standing, we didn't know why-- men, women, the whole camp. And since it happened quite often, so we didn't know why, but we stood there. And the megaphones, the German put on German songs and, among them, German lullabies. I never forget it as long as I live.

speaker: interviewer
text: And all of a sudden, as we were, in this roll call-- the roll-call square was like below, surrounded by the hills. It was a hilly area over there. All of a sudden, we see trucks going on the hills in front of us, you know?

speaker: interviewee
text: At first, we didn't realize it. Then, all of a sudden, we hear screaming and little hands waving. Those were the children taken from the kinder, so-called. They had like a kindergarten for the children. And parents had to go to work. And the children were not allowed to be alone in the barracks. So they formed some kind-- the Germans said, oh, the children will be fed better there. And they will have peace and quiet and other children to play with. And the children were supposed to be in that kindergarten. And the whole kinder, while we were all down there, standing on the roll-call square, they took all the children and took them away.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where they took them?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, to Auschwitz.

speaker: interviewer
text: All right. You heard already?

speaker: interviewee
text: Pardon me?

speaker: interviewer
text: You heard already about Auschwitz in Plaszow?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Oh, definitely, yeah. There were already people taken to Auschwitz from ghetto. Yeah, about Auschwitz we knew. We didn't believe that people were guests there.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah, but how you hear that? How did you heard about Auschwitz?

speaker: interviewee
text: By the word of mouth. That's just how it goes. Everybody knew. You know, sometimes a German could say something. We knew. We knew about all of them. But we didn't believe that they were killing.

speaker: interviewee
text: For instance, my mother-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] my mother was taken from the city of Tarnow, in 1942, to Belzec. You see? We knew about Belzec already in 1942. This was in the ghetto yet. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] So the selections-- selections, what you're saying, so you always passed? You was never selected?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, I was-- either they demanded only the work permit and some of them the health. You know, they saw a young, healthy woman. Mostly, they took-- but on the other hand, they took a lot of younger people, too. It-- it was a whim-- whim of a-- of a German, what he wanted to do at that particular one. They were completely controlling our fate-- completely.

speaker: interviewer
text: So, and that was now we are already the winter of 1943, '44.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you were still working outside? I mean--

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no. I was working, at Madritsch, inside, in that factory that was making those overalls for the Germans. And one day-- one day, one of the-- you met him, by the way.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember when we were in-- can I say that? When we were in New York now, a young man came to you. And you were talking to him. And you were talking about Croatia at the time. Yeah.

speaker: interviewee
text: Remember? He used to work with me, he and his friend. I was-- and at that time, I was sewing on the machine already. And my foreman, Mr. Kaufman, used to show everybody my overalls to show everybody how you're not supposed to sew. [LAUGHS] I was terrible. But anyway. So this Ludwig, Lewis Fagen, his name is now. He and his friend were working near me. And one day, Ludwig says to me, Mila, I know that you know Schindler. Because we knew everything about Mr. Schindler having his factory outside of Plaszow. We knew many, many people who worked for him. And it was like a place, you know, like-- like God-given place, like a paradise comparing to what we were living through. And Ludwig said to me, look, the Russian front is approaching, and Plaszow will be liquidated. So-- and Mr. Schindler has to close his factory.

speaker: interviewer
text: So some kind of a list is being formed now. And since Poldek, your husband, knows him from the very beginning, try to get on the list. So till today, whenever I see Ludwig, I say that he saved my life. Because we didn't do a thing about it. I don't think we knew about the list. And somehow, Poldek passed the word, on a little slip of paper, in the Jewish offices-- to the Jewish offices of the-- first, he asked Marcel Goldberg, who was in charge of making the list, more or less.

speaker: interviewee
text: This was a member of the administration of the camp, Jewish administration. And he refused. He said, he doesn't want-- he cannot do it. And then Poldek passed a little slip of paper to Mr. Schindler. And I think that Mr. Schindler personally approved us on that list. This was the day before they were-- the men left that we got on the list, in the very last moment.

speaker: interviewer
text: But you was working-- you heard about--

speaker: interviewee
text: From a guy who was working with me at Madritsch.

speaker: interviewer
text: No, no, before you went-- before you went on the list, there was-- and when the Russian came, there was some--

speaker: interviewee
text: No, the-- the Russian didn't come yet. The Russian were approaching.

speaker: interviewer
text: Approaching? But then they start to make some escalation in the camp.

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yes. Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you tell us something about it?

speaker: interviewee
text: The Germans wanted to completely erase any traces of their murders. Since our camp didn't have the crematoria, in other camps they just bombed the crematoria, you know, or-- But in our case, people were buried in the ground surrounding that hill on which people were shot. So--

speaker: interviewer
text: What was the name of the hill?

speaker: interviewee
text: It's a very ugly word they used to call it. I cannot say. Poldek will tell you later.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: [LAUGHS] It had a very dirty name. This is our people called it. The-- the hill didn't have any name, but our people christened it that way, you know? Anyway, so the order came. The Germans made us excavate all the bodies-- possibly, I don't ever think they could be all the bodies-- but thousands of bodies, and burned them.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you was working personally there?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not me. I worked at Madritsch. And we were, thank God, protected. But going back from Madritsch, going through the-- what was called the Gelaende, through the torn-down barrack, first of all, we smelled it. It was completely choking us. And we saw people pushing the-- the little carts, with the bodies in it, yeah. I didn't see the excavation, per se, but I saw people pushing those barrels.

speaker: interviewer
text: There was a lot of smoke there?

speaker: interviewee
text: Choking us.

speaker: interviewer
text: [SIGHS] So you went-- you get on the list. And so the camp was slowly--

speaker: interviewee
text: So we go on the list. And two days later, the men go, the transport of men. And we supposed to follow them. We're told--

speaker: interviewer
text: The men that are on the list?

speaker: interviewee
text: The men who are on the list, 800.

speaker: interviewer
text: But what happened to the rest? What happened to the rest of the prisoners in the camp?

speaker: interviewee
text: The rest of the prisoners of the camp were either stayed in Plaszow, if they were needed, if they had the work permits, which were-- showed that they were needed, you know. They stayed till the liquidation. And then, they had this famous death march, where they chased people outside of Plaszow. And they marched for hours and days and days, hungry, beaten, barefoot in the snow and everything. We were very lucky.

speaker: interviewer
text: So your men are left.

speaker: interviewee
text: The men left. We didn't know what--

speaker: interviewer
text: 800 Of them?

speaker: interviewee
text: We thought they're going to Bruennlitz, because Mr. Schindler established-- since he had to liquidate his factory near Cracow, he established another factory in his hometown, near his hometown, of Zwittau, in the German-occupied Czechoslovakia. And he-- we thought, all of us thought, naturally, the men, who are on the list, going directly to Mr. Schindler.

speaker: interviewer
text: Later, we found out that the men were taken to Gross-Rosen instead, kept there for, I think, about three days, and then sent to Schindler. And when they came to Mr. Schindler, they asked, where are our women?

speaker: interviewee
text: At the time, he didn't know where we were. We left a week late-- after our men left. And we also thought we're going directly--

speaker: interviewer
text: How you left?

speaker: interviewee
text: By cattle trains. You know, crowded, with one like a pail for a toilet. No water, no water completely. And this was November. And November, in Poland, you know, beginning of November, already it's cold.

speaker: interviewer
text: November of '44?

speaker: interviewee
text: '44, yeah. And we got on the trains. And so happy, we are going. You know, we're going to join our men. And we're going to be with Mr. Schindler. We were very happy. Instead-- I don't remember how long we went. Must have been-- I remember night. That I remember. And in the middle of the night, we arrived in what we found out was Auschwitz.

speaker: interviewer
text: And under the railing-- how do you say? It was not a station. You know, in the middle of nowhere? [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Groups of Germans, SS, with the dogs, screaming and shouting, and the Jewish prisoners. And they took us to a-- what they called a sauna. Mila, tell me, how you knew that you are in Auschwitz? You knew? How you feel? How--

speaker: interviewee
text: I think that we found out. We asked. There were Slovak-- [PHONE RINGING]

speaker: interviewer
text: Oy. Just tell me. There was what?

speaker: interviewee
text: Slovak prisoners--

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewee
text: --who were there from the very beginning, you know, already very-- and also kind of cruel, most of them, you know? And they told us. We asked them, where are we? What are we doing here? There were women and men helping us to go down the train and taking us to the sauna, where we had to disrobe completely and-- and take everything off. And I don't know what the-- what criteria they used for shaving heads, because, in our transport of 300 Schindler women, some were shaven and some had their hair short-- cut very short. Mine was cut very short. But I had no shoes. Then we went to the next--

speaker: interviewer
text: But it was snowing already?

speaker: interviewee
text: This was already not snowing but lots of mud. Auschwitz is built on a-- I don't know that ground.

speaker: interviewer
text: You're talking about Auschwitz or Birkenau?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I-- [LAUGHS] I didn't even know. I thought I am in Auschwitz. But after the war, when I came to Auschwitz for the first time, you know, when I came to Auschwitz for the first time, I thought to myself-- and we took a guided tour with our daughter and my husband. And I thought, this is not where I was. But then, when we went to Birkenau, surely enough, I knew where I was, you know, because this was Birkenau. Birkenau-- we-- I had wooden clogs. Most of the women had wooden clogs. No underwear, no stockings, nothing. I had some shirts from some men. I think it was men's shirts and some-- some rags on me-- nothing. And it was very, very cold already. And the clogs stuck in the mud. You know, you-- you walked and then-- [SIGHS]

speaker: interviewer
text: So we were very crowded. I remember, we were sleeping, 13 of us, on the bunk, covered with one little coverlet, from which all that cotton was coming out, You know, all dirty. And latrine was like, maybe, at least three blocks away. Yeah. You--

speaker: interviewee
text: And we were terribly hungry, but we couldn't eat even what was given to us, because we were all sick with dysentery.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you saw something. You heard about the burning people, about the gassing.

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, we knew it, because the-- the-- we could see the sky kind of reddish, you know. And-- and so, you know, over there, again, everybody tells us what's happening. You know, everybody knew.

speaker: interviewer
text: So what you were thinking? Now you--

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I was thinking that this is our end. I did not expect us to ever be rescued by Mr. Schindler or being able to get away from this hell. We-- I thought, this is another day or two or three, and we'll be all finished.

speaker: interviewer
text: Because we did not work. I don't know what we were designated for. We were all sick, you know? And because there were some places where people worked, cleaned this, did that, you know? Not our transport. We stayed in the barrack. How was-- in this time-- in this time, how was your faith? You-- you-- how you-- you believed that--

speaker: interviewee
text: I tell you, I always believed in God, even without my formal Jewish education. But I knew there is a God, because my parents always believed, especially my mother, I think. You know? And I always believed. I always say my prayers till today. I-- I-- I have a little conversation with God. So, you know.

speaker: interviewer
text: How you explain that, that all happened to the Jewish people in front of you?

speaker: interviewee
text: I tell you, I don't know why. And I don't want to analyze it. Because if I would analyze it, I would lose, probably, my faith. But you know, it's funny. In-- [SIGHS] was it in the ghetto? No, no, it could not be in the ghetto. In-- in Auschwitz-- yeah. No, I don't remember. I think it was in Auschwitz. I had two bunk mates. You know, they were two girls from a very small town in Poland. Twins, but they didn't look at all like each other. And when we were ready, when they came to take us, you know, they notified us that-- they told our whole group to get together. And they-- the told us we're going on the train. We still didn't know if we're going to Mr. Schindler.

speaker: interviewer
text: And these girl-- girls, the two girls, started to pray, Shema Yisrael. You know this is a prayer, the Jewish Hebrew prayer to God, may have mercy on us and so on. That's the first time I ever heard it, you know?

speaker: interviewee
text: And I-- I thought, what a wonderful thing it is to have faith like this in the middle of this hell, to pray like they did. Yeah. And then they took us on the trains. And the conditions were even worse when we arrived-- when we arrived from Plaszow to Auschwitz. Terrible, and we were exhausted. And we were-- we-- we were all very sick, still, you know, and cold. And then we arriving after maybe two nights and one day, or vice versa. No water. No food. Nothing. We arriving--

speaker: interviewer
text: It was winter, right? That was in December already.

speaker: interviewee
text: This was already-- no, no, no. This was still-- oh, it must have been December, because we were three weeks in Auschwitz. So it must have been December already. Very cold. And we arriving in Bruennlitz. We still don't know that this is Bruennlitz. I think that somebody saw the sign, because we arrived through the station, you know, not in the middle of nowhere but at a train station. And they told us to march toward the camp by fives. And we see, from far away, big, big, tall chimneys. So one of the girls, who was marching with me-- and I remember her. I just-- we found each other after so many years. She was a Jewish girl from Germany. She says to me, Mila, they're going to gas us now. I said-- Margot Schlesinger. I said, Margot, if they wanted to gas us, they could have done it in Auschwitz.

speaker: interviewer
text: Why take us for two days, and God knows how long? And they could have done it now? I-- I-- I was always optimistic. I don't know what-- it's my nature, I guess. So we came. And we came to a factory. It was a big building with a balcony in front of it and a little courtyard. And as we marched-- and we probably looked like a-- like a group of-- of ghosts.

speaker: interviewee
text: Because, on the way out of Auschwitz, they painted our rags in all kinds of colors, because they didn't want us to escape. If an occasion would arise, they knew we could not escape among the regular population with our clothes being painted.

speaker: interviewer
text: And we came. That whole group comes into the courtyard. Then whom do we see there? Mr. Schindler, surrounded by all the SS guards, women and men guards, and the new camp commandant. Leipold was his name.

speaker: interviewee
text: And Mr. Schindler greeted us like long-lost relatives. He said, welcome. Don't worry about anything. Now you are with me. And you going to be OK. Don't be afraid. Soup and bread are inside. Go and help yourself. And we knew, [SIGHS] at that particular moment, that we will be saved. If he can do it, we believe he will do it. We had so much confidence in Schindler.

speaker: interviewer
text: You find your husband there?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, [LAUGHS] most important. As we walk in, this group of ghosts, we see, on the , balcony, we see a group of men. And it was hard for us to recognize our men, because, when we parted in Plaszow, they were all still wearing their own clothes. They did not wear the striped uniform.

speaker: interviewer
text: Here, they are in the striped uniform. Their heads are almost shaven but completely shaven in the middle-- completely. We used to call it a lice promenade, you know? Why did they do it? Because again, if somebody should escape, it's very difficult to escape with a-- Why you call it lice promenade?

speaker: interviewee
text: [LAUGHS] Because the lice couldn't walk there. I don't know. They walked in the hair. I don't know. Anyway, so the men are standing there. And they waving. And they shouting our names.

speaker: interviewer
text: And I see my husband. And it was, of course, wonderful. It was-- we did not stay together. The men had separate quarters. And the women had separate. Those were not barracks. They were like two big halls, for men, two big halls for women, you know? And the latrine was downstairs. When we came, the camp was not ready. We did not have bunks. So we slept on the straw. With Mr. Schindler, we didn't mind anything. Everything was terrific, you know? Even that soup was terrific, which he prepared for us.

speaker: interviewee
text: And somehow, we started to work. The work was really very-- it was a big sabotage, because we really didn't do a thing. And next to our factory was a factory, a wool factory. And our men used to go and steal the wool and made us, on the machines in the factory, the knitting needles, you know. And everybody was knitting, you know, the sweaters, and-- because we didn't have any clothes, for our men and for ourselves. And the life was much easier. But we didn't know how it will end. Because, you know, Schindler could not-- our lagerkommandant, Leipold, was very cruel. And he really wanted to follow the orders from Germany, which came later.

speaker: interviewer
text: We were there seven months or eight months. And toward the end, the orders came, again, to shoot the prisoners. And they had some Russian prisoners of war. And they dug already the trenches for us, you know?

speaker: interviewee
text: But somehow, Mr. Schindler arranged to forge a document and send Leipold to the front. A document came calling Leipold to the front. And he left. And then, we were left with just SS guards, men and women. And they realized it was the end of the war. So somehow, we were saved.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember, maybe, Schindler's wife in this time?

speaker: interviewee
text: Of course, I remember her.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you tell us something--

speaker: interviewee
text: I never knew her. But she was like a-- like a good angel walking among us, you know? I never spoke to her until way after the war. And well, I didn't speak with-- with Mr. Schindler either. He was head director. We all said, hello. And that was about it. And she was like an angel.

speaker: interviewee
text: I had a friend, the sister of this gentleman, who put-- helped me go-- get on the list. Remember, I told you? His sister came already from Auschwitz very sick. It came-- we found out later, it was a cancer of the bone. And she was the only person who died in Mr. Schindler's camp.

speaker: interviewee
text: And Mrs. Schindler, she was put in a-- they improvised a little-- [COUGHING] excuse me-- a little hospital, downstairs. And she was kept there. And Mrs. Schindler was feeding her Cream of Wheat, white bread, you know, anything to make her better. Yeah, she was wonderful, Mrs. Schindler. She did it in her own very quiet way.

speaker: interviewer
text: May I?

speaker: interviewee
text: [COUGHING] [DRINKS]

speaker: interviewer
text: [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] So, then, that was now already in 1945. And now, the liberation came. How were you-- what is your--

speaker: interviewee
text: You know, it was an eerie feeling. You know, in front of our camp, there was a big, big, almost a mountain, like a big hill. And being in the camp, we always said, what are we going to do? If we will be free, what's beyond that hill? What will we do, you know? And when the liberation came, we went beyond the hill.

speaker: interviewer
text: But before you went behind the hill, what's happened with Schindler? And what happened with the SS? What happened with you? How are you--

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, the SS-- the SS either fled or was disarmed by our people. Because Mr. Schindler, also, besides giving us some extra bread, which was in the storerooms, you know, prepared for us, clothing materials, all kinds. Every one of us got several pieces of fabric. So we could either barter it, for something else, or sell it, or make something for ourselves. So each one had a little capital with which to start their new life, thanks to Mr. Schindler. Also, there was a-- a storeroom full of cigarettes, which he was able to-- to manage.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember the night-- the night before you were free?

speaker: interviewee
text: When he left, I remember the night, distinctly. But I wasn't there. Because I was probably in the barrack. I don't remember. But I know my husband was there and several other people. And-- but I remember the speech that he had to us just on the day of liberation. And we heard the Winston Churchill speech. And Mr. Schindler spoke to us. And-- and really, he spoke beautifully and told us that we will start a new life and, hopefully, find our relatives, and so on, so.

speaker: interviewer
text: So what happened in the morning with that hill?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, to the hill. So we decided we going to the town, because, you know, our camp was right in the town of Bruennlitz. This was a German name. Czech name is Brnenec. And we get to the town, me in my rags-- everybody like that-- and the-- the clogs. And on the streets, there are many, many partisans, young Czech boys. And they cry when they see us. And they're so nice. And all of a sudden, I see-- we see-- I-- one of these partisans stopped us. And he started to talk to us and wanted to know where we come from and about the our life and everything. And all of a sudden, I see two German women are coming. He's stopping them. And he says to one of them, take off your shoes. Let her try it.

speaker: interviewee
text: And me, with my European good manners, I said, no, no, no. Please, don't do that. I felt terrible. I felt so bad, you know? I shouldn't have felt bad, because these people took everything from us, what we had.

speaker: interviewee
text: And this woman took off her shoes. And he made me put them on. And she took the clogs. And then we're walking a few-- maybe a few meters. The woman is following us. And if you think that I didn't give her back her shoes, and she gave me the clogs. But she said she has a villa. All these Germans were very, very well-to-do. She has a villa. And she was afraid. The town was full of Russians. She was afraid the Russians will come and throw her out of the villa, you know, occupy the villa. And-- but having us there, all we had to say that we are from concentration camp, and the Russians left us completely alone.

speaker: interviewee
text: So she invited us to stay in her house. And she was probably a good Nazi. She moved out someplace else. And the Russians stayed downstairs. And we stayed upstairs. And it was wonderful. Sorry, you may--
